title: Conjugate Pairs, Amphiprotic Species and Water
subtitle: How one substance can be both acid and base
subject: Chemistry
syllabusDotPoints: write ionic equations to represent the dissociation of acids and bases in water, conjugate acid/base pairs in solution and the amphiprotic nature of some salts, for example sodium hydrogen carbonate, NaHCO‚āÉ, and potassium dihydrogen phosphate, KH‚āāPO‚āĄ
inquiryQuestion: What is the role of water in solutions of acids and bases?
lessonIntent: Students can identify conjugate pairs, write equations for amphiprotic salts as both acid and base, and use Kw to relate [H‚āÉO‚Āļ] and [OH‚ĀĽ].
examSkill: Find conjugate pairs (one proton apart); write both amphiprotic equations for HCO‚āÉ‚ĀĽ, H‚āāPO‚āĄ‚ĀĽ, HPO‚āĄ¬≤‚ĀĽ; apply Kw and pH + pOH = 14 at 25 ¬įC.

introVoiceover.text: Welcome to HSC Science. This is HSC Chemistry. Today, conjugate pairs, amphiprotic species, and water's role. Let's get into it.

scenes.caption: Conjugate Pairs, Amphiprotic Species and Water (Year 12 Chemistry, Module 6, Lesson 7)

scenes.heading: One compound, two behaviours
scenes.body: Add baking soda to acid and it fizzes, easing the acidity. Add the same baking soda to a base and it eases the basicity.
scenes.callout: How can one substance act as a base, then as an acid?
scenes.caption: NaHCO‚āÉ neutralises both an acid and a base, hinting at amphiprotic behaviour.

voiceover.text: OK, here's a small mystery from the lab bench. A chemist takes baking soda, sodium hydrogen carbonate, and adds it to some hydrochloric acid. It fizzes vigorously and the solution becomes less acidic. Then she takes the very same baking soda, the same amount, and adds it to sodium hydroxide. This time the solution becomes less basic, and there are no bubbles. The same compound made one solution less acidic and another less basic. So how can a single substance behave as a base in one reaction and as an acid in another? That's not a contradiction, it's a clue, and by the end you'll explain it cleanly. We'll also meet water doing exactly the same double act on itself.

scenes.heading: Conjugate pairs, one proton apart
scenes.body: Donate a proton and you become your conjugate base; accept one and you become your conjugate acid.

bullets.text: Conjugate base: the acid minus one H‚Āļ, so one more negative charge.
Conjugate acid: the base plus one H‚Āļ, so one less negative charge.
Inverse strength: a strong acid has a very weak conjugate base.

scenes.secondary: Remove only one proton at a time. The conjugate base of H‚āāSO‚āĄ is HSO‚āĄ‚ĀĽ, and HSO‚āĄ‚ĀĽ's conjugate base is SO‚āĄ¬≤‚ĀĽ, two separate pairs.
scenes.callout: Ka √ó Kb = Kw ties the strengths of a conjugate pair together.
scenes.caption: Conjugate base = acid ‚ąí H‚Āļ; conjugate acid = base + H‚Āļ; one proton at a time.

voiceover.text: Let's deepen conjugate pairs from lesson one. When an acid donates its proton, what's left is its conjugate base, with one fewer hydrogen and one more negative charge. When a base accepts a proton, it becomes its conjugate acid, with one more hydrogen and one less negative charge. The pair always differs by exactly one proton and sits on opposite sides of the equation. There's also a strength rule, an inverse one, a strong acid gives up its proton so readily that the leftover conjugate base barely wants it back, so strong acid means very weak conjugate base. Quantitatively, Ka times Kb equals Kw for any conjugate pair. And the classic trap, remove only one proton at a time. The conjugate base of sulfuric acid is hydrogen sulfate, and only then does hydrogen sulfate's conjugate base become sulfate. Two separate pairs, never collapse them into one.

scenes.heading: Amphiprotic: acid or base on demand
scenes.body: An amphiprotic species has an ionisable H‚Āļ and a site to accept one, so its role depends on its partner.

bullets.text: HCO‚āÉ‚ĀĽ donates: HCO‚āÉ‚ĀĽ ‚áĆ H‚Āļ + CO‚āÉ¬≤‚ĀĽ, acting as an acid.
HCO‚āÉ‚ĀĽ accepts: HCO‚āÉ‚ĀĽ + H‚Āļ ‚áĆ H‚āāCO‚āÉ, acting as a base.
NESA names two salts: Na‚āāHPO‚āĄ gives HPO‚āĄ¬≤‚ĀĽ; KH‚āāPO‚āĄ gives H‚āāPO‚āĄ‚ĀĽ.

scenes.secondary: Use the phosphate chain. To act as an acid, step right and lose H‚Āļ; to act as a base, step left and gain H‚Āļ. One proton per equation.
scenes.callout: Amphiprotic is about protons; amphoteric is the broader Lewis idea.
scenes.caption: Amphiprotic species (HCO‚āÉ‚ĀĽ, H‚āāPO‚āĄ‚ĀĽ, HPO‚āĄ¬≤‚ĀĽ) act as acid or base by one proton step.

voiceover.text: Now the star of the lesson, amphiprotic species. A substance is amphiprotic if it can both donate a proton and accept one, depending on its partner. That needs two things, at least one ionisable hydrogen, and a site that can take a proton. The hydrogen carbonate ion is the classic case. It can donate, hydrogen carbonate in balance with hydrogen ion plus carbonate, acting as an acid. Or it can accept, hydrogen carbonate plus hydrogen ion in balance with carbonic acid, acting as a base. NESA names two salts you must know, sodium hydrogen phosphate, which gives the H-P-O-four two-minus ion, and potassium dihydrogen phosphate, which gives the H two P-O-four minus ion. The trick is to picture the phosphate chain. To act as an acid, step one place right, losing a proton. To act as a base, step one place left, gaining a proton. Always exactly one proton per equation. And don't confuse amphiprotic, which is about protons, with amphoteric, the broader Lewis idea.

scenes.heading: The words you need

bullets.text: Amphiprotic: can donate or accept a proton, like HCO‚āÉ‚ĀĽ and H‚āāO.
Autoionisation of water: 2H‚āāO ‚áĆ H‚āÉO‚Āļ + OH‚ĀĽ, to a tiny extent.
Neutral: [H‚āÉO‚Āļ] = [OH‚ĀĽ], which is pH 7 only at 25 ¬įC.

scenes.callout: Neutral is defined by equal ion concentrations, not by the number 7.
scenes.caption: Amphiprotic, autoionisation of water, Kw, neutral.

voiceover.text: Quick vocabulary. Amphiprotic means a species can donate or accept a proton depending on its partner, like hydrogen carbonate and water. Autoionisation of water is water reacting with itself, two water in balance with hydronium and hydroxide, which happens to a tiny extent. K-w is the special equilibrium constant for that, the product of hydronium and hydroxide concentrations, equal to one times ten to the minus fourteen at twenty-five degrees. And neutral means hydronium and hydroxide concentrations are equal. That's the real definition, and it only happens to land at pH seven at twenty-five degrees. At body temperature, neutral is closer to pH six-point-eight. So neutral is about equal ion concentrations, not about the number seven.

scenes.heading: Water ionises itself
scenes.body: Even ultra-pure water carries a trace of ions, because water transfers a proton to itself.

bullets.text: 2H‚āāO ‚áĆ H‚āÉO‚Āļ + OH‚ĀĽ: one water is the acid, the other the base.
Kw = [H‚āÉO‚Āļ][OH‚ĀĽ] = 1.0 √ó 10‚ĀĽ¬Ļ‚Āī at 25 ¬įC, so each is 10‚ĀĽ‚Ā∑ in pure water.
Kw rises with temperature, since the self-ionisation is endothermic.

scenes.secondary: Taking ‚ąílog of Kw gives pH + pOH = 14 at 25 ¬įC, the route to every base pH calculation in the next lesson.
scenes.callout: Kw applies in every aqueous solution, not just pure water.
scenes.caption: 2H‚āāO ‚áĆ H‚āÉO‚Āļ + OH‚ĀĽ; Kw = 1.0 √ó 10‚ĀĽ¬Ļ‚Āī; pH + pOH = 14 at 25 ¬įC.

voiceover.text: Now water's own party trick, and it confirms water is amphiprotic. In eighteen ninety-three, Kohlrausch purified water as far as he could and measured a tiny but real conductivity, which meant ions had to be present. The only source was water itself, ionising. We write it as two water in balance with hydronium and hydroxide, where one water donates a proton and acts as the acid, and the other accepts it and acts as the base, in the same reaction. The equilibrium constant is K-w, the product of hydronium and hydroxide concentrations, one times ten to the minus fourteen at twenty-five degrees. So in pure water each ion is ten to the minus seven molar, giving pH seven. And Kw rises with temperature, because the self-ionisation is endothermic. Take the negative log of Kw, and you get pH plus pOH equals fourteen at twenty-five degrees, the key to next lesson's base calculations. Remember, Kw applies in every aqueous solution, even concentrated acid.

scenes.heading: Amphiprotic equations
scenes.question: For KH‚āāPO‚āĄ, write H‚āāPO‚āĄ‚ĀĽ acting as an acid and as a base. Name the conjugate in each.
scenes.coachNote: Use the chain. Acid = step right (lose H‚Āļ); base = step left (gain H‚Āļ).
steps: As an acid, step right, lose H‚Āļ: H‚āāPO‚āĄ‚ĀĽ ‚áĆ H‚Āļ + HPO‚āĄ¬≤‚ĀĽ (conjugate base HPO‚āĄ¬≤‚ĀĽ)
As a base, step left, gain H‚Āļ: H‚āāPO‚āĄ‚ĀĽ + H‚Āļ ‚áĆ H‚āÉPO‚āĄ (conjugate acid H‚āÉPO‚āĄ)
Both use ‚áĆ because these are partial, weak processes
scenes.caption: H‚āāPO‚āĄ‚ĀĽ as acid ‚Üí HPO‚āĄ¬≤‚ĀĽ; as base ‚Üí H‚āÉPO‚āĄ.

voiceover.text: Let's write the two equations NESA asks for. Potassium dihydrogen phosphate dissolves into potassium ions, which are just spectators, and the dihydrogen phosphate ion, which is amphiprotic. First, as an acid, we step one place right in the chain, losing a proton, so dihydrogen phosphate in balance with hydrogen ion plus hydrogen phosphate, and the conjugate base is hydrogen phosphate, H-P-O-four two-minus. Second, as a base, we step one place left, gaining a proton, so dihydrogen phosphate plus hydrogen ion in balance with phosphoric acid, and the conjugate acid is phosphoric acid. Both use the equilibrium arrow, because these are partial, weak processes. So acting as an acid it becomes hydrogen phosphate, and acting as a base it becomes phosphoric acid. One proton each way, just one step along the chain.

scenes.heading: Using Kw
scenes.question: At 25 ¬įC a solution has [OH‚ĀĽ] = 2.0 √ó 10‚ĀĽ¬≥ mol L‚ĀĽ¬Ļ. Find [H‚āÉO‚Āļ] and state if it is acidic or basic.
scenes.coachNote: Kw is a fixed product, so rearrange for the unknown ion.
steps: Answer: [H‚āÉO‚Āļ] = 5.0 √ó 10‚ĀĽ¬Ļ¬≤ mol L‚ĀĽ¬Ļ, so the solution is basic ‚úď
scenes.caption: [H‚āÉO‚Āļ] = Kw/[OH‚ĀĽ] = 5.0 √ó 10‚ĀĽ¬Ļ¬≤ mol L‚ĀĽ¬Ļ; OH‚ĀĽ dominates, so basic.

voiceover.text: Now a quick Kw calculation. At twenty-five degrees a solution has a hydroxide concentration of two times ten to the minus three molar. Find the hydronium concentration and say whether it's acidic or basic. Step one, Kw is the product of hydronium and hydroxide, fixed at one times ten to the minus fourteen. Step two, rearrange, hydronium equals Kw divided by hydroxide, one times ten to the minus fourteen over two times ten to the minus three. Step three, that's five times ten to the minus twelve molar. Step four, compare the two, hydroxide at two times ten to the minus three is vastly bigger than hydronium at five times ten to the minus twelve. So hydroxide dominates and the solution is basic. Notice you never needed the actual base, Kw alone links the two ion concentrations in any aqueous solution.

scenes.heading: Three traps
scenes.body: Trap 1: calling SO‚āĄ¬≤‚ĀĽ the conjugate base of H‚āāSO‚āĄ. Remove one proton, so it's HSO‚āĄ‚ĀĽ.
scenes.secondary: Trap 2: thinking neutral always means pH 7. Neutral means [H‚āÉO‚Āļ] = [OH‚ĀĽ]; at 37 ¬įC that's about pH 6.8. Trap 3: saying HPO‚āĄ¬≤‚ĀĽ can't donate a proton because it's negative. It still has an O‚ÄďH bond and can donate to a strong enough base.
scenes.mistakeTag: Where conjugate and Kw marks slip
scenes.callout: One proton at a time; neutral isn't always 7; charge doesn't block donation.
scenes.caption: Conjugate base of H‚āāSO‚āĄ is HSO‚āĄ‚ĀĽ; neutral ‚Č† always 7; negative ions can still donate H‚Āļ.

voiceover.text: Three traps. Trap one, calling sulfate the conjugate base of sulfuric acid. A conjugate base is one proton removed, so it's hydrogen sulfate. Sulfate is the conjugate base of hydrogen sulfate, a separate pair. Always one proton at a time. Trap two, assuming neutral always means pH seven. Neutral is defined by hydronium concentration equalling hydroxide concentration, and that only gives pH seven at twenty-five degrees. At body temperature, thirty-seven degrees, Kw is bigger, so neutral water sits near pH six-point-eight, and a solution at exactly pH seven there is actually slightly basic. Trap three, thinking hydrogen phosphate two-minus can't donate a proton because it's already negatively charged. Charge doesn't block donation. It still holds an oxygen-hydrogen bond, and it can hand that proton to a strong enough base to become phosphate.

scenes.heading: Quick check
scenes.question: At 37 ¬įC, Kw = 2.4 √ó 10‚ĀĽ¬Ļ‚Āī. A solution measures pH 7.0. Is it acidic, basic, or neutral?
answerSteps: The solution's pH is 7.0, which is above the neutral 6.81
Answer: the solution is slightly basic ‚úď
scenes.caption: At 37 ¬įC neutral is pH 6.81, so pH 7.0 is slightly basic.

voiceover.text: Quick check, and it's a favourite exam twist. At thirty-seven degrees, Kw is two-point-four times ten to the minus fourteen. A solution measures pH seven. Is it acidic, basic, or neutral? Pause and work it through. Right, let's check it. First, find the neutral pH at this temperature, which is minus the log of the square root of Kw. The square root of two-point-four times ten to the minus fourteen is one-point-five-five times ten to the minus seven, and minus the log of that is about six-point-eight-one. So at thirty-seven degrees, neutral is pH six-point-eight-one, not seven. Our solution is at pH seven, which is above the neutral six-point-eight-one. A higher pH than neutral means hydroxide outweighs hydronium. So the solution is slightly basic. Don't assume pH seven is neutral unless you're at twenty-five degrees.

scenes.heading: Lock it in
points: Conjugate pairs differ by one H‚Āļ; strong acid ‚Üí very weak conjugate base.
Remove one proton at a time: H‚āāSO‚āĄ ‚Üí HSO‚āĄ‚ĀĽ ‚Üí SO‚āĄ¬≤‚ĀĽ.
Amphiprotic species act as acid or base; write both equations.
scenes.finalPrompt: Amphiprotic ion? Write it both ways, one proton per equation.
scenes.caption: Five takeaways: conjugate pairs, one-proton rule, amphiprotic equations, Kw, neutral‚Č†always 7.

voiceover.text: Let's lock it in. Five things to take away. First, conjugate pairs differ by exactly one proton, and a strong acid always has a very weak conjugate base. Second, remove one proton at a time, so sulfuric acid goes to hydrogen sulfate, then hydrogen sulfate goes to sulfate. Third, an amphiprotic species can act as an acid or a base, and the exam wants both equations, one proton each. Fourth, water self-ionises, with Kw the product of hydronium and hydroxide, fixed at one times ten to the minus fourteen at twenty-five degrees. And fifth, neutral genuinely means equal hydronium and hydroxide, which only lands at pH seven at twenty-five degrees. Next lesson, we turn Kw into real numbers, calculating pH and pOH for strong acids and bases. See you there.
